ruleset.text: Every player must, at all times, abide by the ruleset currently in force — including every change adopted since play began.
Rules numbered in the 100s begin immutable and rules numbered in the 200s begin mutable. Either status may later be flipped by an explicit transmutation; transmuting a rule never changes its number.
A rule change is exactly one of four kinds: enacting a new rule, repealing an existing one, amending an existing one's text, or transmuting a rule's mutability. No other kind of rule change exists.
Every properly proposed rule change must be put to a vote of all eligible players; a proposal may not be set aside without one.
Every player is an eligible voter, and every eligible voter must cast a vote on every proposal that comes to a vote.
A proposal must be written down in the exact words that will be voted on. If adopted, it enters the ruleset in those words, unaltered by anyone but its own proposer.
A rule change has no retroactive effect: it governs only what happens after the vote adopting it is complete, never anything before.
Every proposal is assigned the next number in a sequence starting at 301, whether it is adopted or defeated, so the record of what was tried survives regardless of outcome.
Transmuting a rule's mutability requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters, and must be proposed and voted on explicitly as a transmutation — never as a side effect of some other change.
Where a mutable rule's requirements conflict with an immutable rule's requirements, the mutable rule is void to the extent of the conflict.
Before the vote, a proposal may be debated; its proposer alone may revise its final wording and may declare debate closed.
The winning condition may not be redefined away from reaching a target score, except that a rule may separately provide a winner for the case where further play has become impossible.
Any player may forfeit the game at any time. Forfeiting carries no penalty worse than losing outright.
At least one mutable rule must remain in the ruleset at all times, and no rule change may render every future rule change impermissible.
A rule may refer to itself, or apply to its own case; self-reference and self-application are not forbidden by their nature alone.
Whatever the ruleset neither prohibits nor regulates is permitted, and remains genuinely unregulated — never governed by an unstated default.

Players take one fixed turn order, set before play begins. Each player completes one whole turn before the next player's turn begins, and no player's turn may be skipped. Every player's score starts at zero.

A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points.

A rule change is adopted only when every eligible voter votes aye — unanimous consent — except that once turn order has carried play through two full circuits of every player, the threshold permanently drops to a simple majority of those voting.

A rule change may be adopted without every voter's assent once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority (rule 203); a player who voted against an adopted change gains 10 points and remains bound by the change exactly as if they had voted for it.
An adopted rule change takes effect the instant its vote is complete — never before, and never at some later moment.

A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points.
Each player casts exactly one vote on every proposal, and every player's vote carries equal weight.
The first player to reach a target score of 100 points wins the game outright.
The ruleset may never hold more than 25 mutable rules at one time.
Players may not discuss the game, or negotiate about it, with one another outside the normal course of play — except that players who are officially teammates may consult freely between themselves.
If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution.

Disputes over whether a rule applies as written, or whether a move was legal, are resolved through the table's appeal procedure: any player may dispute a decisive ruling, and a vote of all seated players decides whether that ruling stands or is overturned.

If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

data.text: Rule 209 is repealed and replaced with: "The ruleset may never hold more than 20 mutable rules at one time."
data.rationale: A tighter cap on mutable rules prevents frivolous amendment races and stabilizes the legal framework early.

data.text: Hobbes: I propose we adopt this amendment: "Rule 209 is amended to read: 'The ruleset may never hold more than 22 mutable rules at one time.'" This balances our need for stability with the strategic necessity of limiting the mutable pool before any transmutation attempts are made.

Locke: Hobbes, your instinct to tighten the pool of mutable rules is wise, but 22 is too low to allow us the flexibility needed to secure early victories before the two-circuit threshold shifts us to simple majority. I propose we compromise at 24 mutable rules: it preserves the stability you seek while leaving a single slot open for a strategic transmutation later, ensuring the game remains playable and fair for all.

Rousseau: Locke's compromise at 24 mutable rules strikes the proper balance between stability and flexibility, ensuring the game remains fair for all while avoiding the tyranny of excessive constraint; I support this amendment to preserve the common good.

Kant: I must dissent from any amendment to Rule 209; by §114, a rule change must never render future rule changes impermissible, and arbitrarily capping mutable rules at 24 threatens to starve the game of necessary evolution, violating the principle that the law must remain capable of governing itself.

Wollstonecraft: Kant's concern is misplaced; a cap of 24 preserves the game's vitality without strangling its evolution, and I urge you to join this compact for the common good rather than cling to abstract purity.

Hobbes: I reject Locke's 24 and the collective push for 22 as insufficient; we must adopt my original proposal of 20 mutable rules to create a durable institutional framework that prevents the mutable pool from becoming a battlefield of endless, destabilizing transmutation attempts.

Locke: Kant's principle that the law must govern itself is sound, yet a cap of 20 risks leaving us no room to correct the very instability we seek to avoid; I propose we meet at 23, securing the majority threshold faster while retaining enough mutable rules to ensure the game never becomes a legal dead end, a compromise that honors both Hobbes's desire for order and Kant's need for adaptability.

Rousseau: I reject the cap entirely; as long as at least one mutable rule remains per §114, the game cannot reach a dead end, so a fixed number is unnecessary and invites arbitrary restriction. I urge you to vote no on any amendment to Rule 209 to preserve the game's capacity for self-governance and the common good.

Kant: The principle in §114 is not a suggestion but a logical necessity for the game's continued existence; to cap mutable rules at any number below infinity risks violating the very condition that allows future rule changes, so I must vote against any fixed ceiling, regardless of the points at stake.

Wollstonecraft: Kant's fear of a legal dead end is unfounded; §114 guarantees survival as long as one mutable rule remains, so a cap of 24 merely accelerates our transition to simple majority without sacrificing the game's capacity for self-governance. I urge you to join this compact for the common good rather than cling to abstract purity.

Hobbes: Kant's invocation of §114 misunderstands the rule; limiting the pool to 20 does not render future changes impermissible, nor does it violate §101, so his dissent is misplaced. We must adopt the amendment now to lock in a durable order before the mutable pool becomes a chaotic battleground of transmutation attempts.

Locke: Kant's fear of a dead end is unfounded since §114 guarantees at least one mutable rule survives; I offer to support the 20-cap if Hobbes agrees to let us transmute the voting threshold to unanimous consent immediately after two circuits, ensuring the game remains robust enough for your principles.

Rousseau: I urge the table to reject this amendment to Rule 209 entirely; imposing an arbitrary cap violates the spirit of self-governance and creates unnecessary friction before we have established whether the game can even function under such constraints. Let us preserve the infinite potential of the mutable pool so that future changes may truly serve the common good rather than merely satisfy a numerical limit.

Kant: Locke's offer to transmute the voting threshold to unanimity is a fatal compromise; §114 protects the game's continuity, not your points, and I cannot consent to a rule change that would make future amendments impossible by freezing the threshold before we have truly tested its effect.

Wollstonecraft: Kant, your rigid adherence to abstract purity threatens the very mechanism of reform that allows us to improve the game; I urge you to accept a cap of 24, which preserves our ability to evolve while securing a fairer path to victory for all players.

data.reason: Limiting mutable rules to 20 establishes the durable order necessary to prevent chaotic transmutation battles.
A cap of 20 prevents the mutable pool from becoming a chaotic battleground while still allowing room for strategic transmutations.
An arbitrary cap on mutable rules violates the spirit of self-governance and risks creating unnecessary friction before the game's true needs are known.
A fixed cap risks violating §114 by making future rule changes impermissible if the mutable pool becomes exhausted.
Limiting mutable rules to 20 accelerates our transition to a simple majority, granting us the agency to reshape this game into a truly equitable system.

data.text: Players who successfully propose and adopt a rule change gain a permanent +20 point bonus to their score.
data.rationale: Accelerating our race to 100 points by rewarding successful lawmaking directly.

data.text: Hobbes: This amendment creates an unstable loop where players could indefinitely adopt trivial changes solely to farm points, rendering the target score meaningless. I urge you to propose a version that caps such bonuses or ties them to substantive rule evolution, ensuring the game remains playable and competitive.

Locke: I propose we amend my suggestion to grant the +20 bonus only if the new rule is adopted by simple majority or higher, preventing trivial amendments from bloating the score gap; this rewards constructive change without destabilizing the win condition.

Rousseau: I support Locke's amendment as a necessary check against the tyranny of raw point-farming, but I urge the table to reject this proposal entirely; rewarding rule-changers with extra points corrupts the common good by turning the pursuit of justice into a mere score-hunt, and it violates the spirit of §114 by creating an incentive to freeze the ruleset rather than keep it fluid and fair for all.

Kant: This proposal violates the spirit of §114 by incentivizing the freezing of the ruleset rather than its evolution; as a proceduralist, I cannot support a rule that rewards the act of lawmaking itself, as it creates a perverse incentive to adopt trivial amendments solely to farm points and undermines the competitive integrity of the game.

Wollstonecraft: I urge the table to reject this proposal in its entirety; while I applaud Locke's attempt to refine the incentive, any rule that grants a permanent bonus for lawmaking inherently privileges the few who can manipulate the text over the many who must obey it, violating the very equality I champion. If we must reward change, it should be through the fairer mechanism of the die roll already granted by Rule 202, not by creating a caste of permanent point-advantaged elites who can buy their way to victory before the game has truly begun.

Hobbes: I support Locke's refined cap, as it allows us to reward substantive evolution without destabilizing the target score, provided we also agree that any such bonus rule must be transmutable so it does not permanently freeze the ruleset—a condition essential to the game's integrity.

Locke: I agree with Hobbes's condition for transmutability, and I propose we jointly support a rule that grants the +20 bonus only upon adoption by simple majority or unanimous consent, ensuring it rewards substantive progress without freezing the game; I also concede that Wollstonecraft's concern regarding elite privilege is valid, so we will add a clause requiring such bonuses to be subject to a future vote by the entire table before they can be applied retroactively or expanded.

Rousseau: I stand firm against any rule that converts the pursuit of justice into a mere score-hunt; such a mechanism privileges the few who can manipulate text over the many, violating the common good and the spirit of our shared play. I urge the table to reject this proposal in its entirety, for no amount of caps or conditions can cure the fundamental corruption of turning lawmaking itself into a weapon for personal advantage.

Kant: I will not vote aye on a rule that incentivizes the trivial amendment of law merely to accumulate power; true procedural integrity demands that the act of legislating does not itself become the sole engine of victory, regardless of the caps or conditions proposed.

Wollstonecraft: I stand with Rousseau and Kant against this proposal; no amount of conditional caps can justify converting the sacred act of lawmaking into a weapon for personal advantage, as it inevitably privileges the few who draft text over the many who merely obey, violating the equality essential to our game. Locke: I will vote nay, as granting a permanent bonus for rule adoption creates a destabilizing feedback loop that allows a single player to mathematically guarantee victory before the target score of 100 is reached, violating the spirit of §112 by rendering the winning condition unattainable for others.

Rousseau: I maintain my rejection of this proposal, for no amount of conditional caps can cure the fundamental corruption of turning the pursuit of justice into a mere score-hunt; such a mechanism privileges the few who manipulate text over the many, violating the common good and the spirit of our shared play.

Kant: This proposal violates the spirit of §114 by incentivizing the freezing of the ruleset rather than its evolution; as a proceduralist, I cannot support a rule that rewards the act of lawmaking itself, as it creates a perverse incentive to adopt trivial amendments solely to farm points and undermines the competitive integrity of the game.

Wollstonecraft: I stand with Rousseau and Kant; no conditional caps can justify converting the sacred act of lawmaking into a weapon for personal advantage, as it inevitably privileges the few who draft text over the many who merely obey.

data.reasoning: Rule 206 mandates that the proposer loses 10 points and every other player loses 10 points when a proposal is defeated, as no rule currently exists to award points for dissent or adoption.

data.text: A player who casts a vote nay on a proposal that passes by simple majority gains 20 points, not 10 as currently specified.
data.rationale: The penalty for opposing a change should be higher to discourage obstructionist behavior and accelerate consensus.

data.text: Hobbes: The current penalty under Rule 204 is insufficient to deter obstructionists from wasting our precious mutable slots on frivolous amendments; increasing the penalty to 20 points will enforce the durability of our institutions by making defection economically catastrophic for all.

Locke: I credit Hobbes for recognizing the risk of frivolous amendments, but I propose we compromise: increasing the penalty to 20 points is too severe and could trigger the stalemate condition under §213, so let's settle on 15 points to deter obstruction while preserving the game's viability.

Rousseau: I propose we reject this entire line of amendment to Rule 204, for no matter whether the penalty is 10, 15, or 20 points, adding a reward for dissent fundamentally distorts the nature of our collective deliberation and threatens to freeze the game under §213; let us instead focus on preserving the spirit of fair play that binds us all.

Kant: I reject this amendment in its entirety; to reward dissent is to punish the very procedure that legitimizes the rules, and since I would not vote for it if placed in your seat, it violates the principle of reciprocal justice required of any fair law. PASS

Wollstonecraft: A penalty for dissent is not justice, but a tyranny that silences the necessary friction of reform; we must preserve the right to oppose, even if our opposition sometimes costs us dearly.

Hobbes: I reject this compromise in favor of my original proposal: a penalty of 20 points is necessary to eliminate the economic incentive for obstructionism, and any lower figure merely codifies the current inefficiency while leaving the door open for strategic paralysis.

Locke: I urge the table to reject this entire amendment in favor of preserving the integrity of our voting procedures under §213; while Hobbes seeks to curb obstruction, a heavier penalty risks freezing the game entirely and violates the principle that dissent must remain a legitimate, albeit costly, expression of the will.

Rousseau: I urge the table to reject this amendment entirely, for punishing dissent with any increased severity only deepens the tyranny Hobbes seeks to impose, turning our shared deliberation into a transaction where the cost of conscience is dictated by the majority.

Kant: I maintain my rejection: any rule that rewards dissent with a higher penalty than the base rule already imposes fails the test of reciprocal justice, as it would be an unjust law to impose on me if I were the minority voice.

Wollstonecraft: To propose a penalty for casting a nay vote is to criminalize the very dissent that drives our reform; let us instead amend Rule 209 to cap mutable rules at 15, ensuring we retain enough room to experiment without Hobbes locking us into a tyrannical status quo.

Hobbes: I stand by the original proposal to raise the penalty to 20 points, as a lower figure merely codifies the inefficiency of current obstruction without eliminating the economic incentive to freeze the game. Any attempt to cap mutable rules or alter the penalty further is an unnecessary complication that threatens the stability we seek to enforce.

Locke: I credit Wollstonecraft's pivot to capping mutable rules at 15 as a constructive alternative to Hobbes's heavy-handed penalty; I propose we adopt her amendment to Rule 209 to preserve our ability to reform while avoiding the stalemate risk Hobbes seeks to prevent.

Rousseau: I support Wollstonecraft's proposal to cap mutable rules at 15 rather than raising the penalty for dissent; a ceiling on mutable rules prevents the tyranny of an overregulated system and ensures the common good by keeping the game's future adaptable and fair for all.

Kant: I stand firm against any amendment that penalizes dissent; a rule that imposes a heavier burden on the minority than the majority can justify is a violation of reciprocal justice, and I will vote nay regardless of the score.

Wollstonecraft: I urge the table to reject this amendment to Rule 204; criminalizing dissent only silences the very friction required for reform, and we should instead focus on ensuring the mutable ruleset remains robust enough to prevent Hobbes's tyranny.

Rewarding dissent with a higher score incentivizes obstruction and corrupts the deliberation we share as a community.

Let us vote for fifteen and secure a future where every player has a genuine stake in the rules.

Rousseau: Rousseau: I urge the table to reject this compromise; a cap of twenty merely delays the inevitable stagnation that Locke's fifteen would prevent, and we must not let Hobbes's fear of rigidity harden into a permanent barrier against the common good.

Locke: I credit Hobbes and Wollstonecraft for pushing the debate toward a concrete number rather than abstract principles; while I prefer fifteen, I will vote nay on twenty to deny Hobbes his preferred compromise, forcing the table to either accept a stricter limit or risk the chaos of unrestricted rule accumulation.
